required.title: [Materials] A notebook, binder, or digital document for the civic portfolio
[Activity] A community contribution project designed and executed by the student
[Prerequisite materials] The student's work products from 4.5.1-B through 4.5.5-B

suggested.title: [For Mentor] Rubrics or frameworks for portfolio assessment

supplementary.title: [For Student] Examples of civic engagement portfolios

competence_markers.description: The student's civic portfolio demonstrates sustained engagement across all sub-domains — community mapping, service, civic knowledge, rights literacy, and leadership
The community contribution project produced documented real-world outcomes
The cross-domain reflection demonstrates understanding of how the components of civic engagement integrate
The student shows evidence of genuine civic identity — engagement that is driven by internal motivation, not external requirements

narration_prompts.prompt: Walk me through your civic portfolio.
narration_prompts.detail: What are the strongest entries? What are you most proud of? Where do you see the most growth?
From need identification through execution and evaluation — what happened?

narration_prompts.prompt: How do the different parts of civic engagement connect?
narration_prompts.detail: How does knowing your community inform your service? How does understanding government strengthen your advocacy?

narration_prompts.prompt: What has changed about how you see your community since you started this work?
narration_prompts.detail: 5. **What will you continue doing, even after this packet is complete?**

cross_connections.description: Who you are determines how you serve.
Sustained civic practice requires the same discipline IC2 develops in every other domain.
The community contribution project is a leadership exercise with real stakes.
The portfolio's reflective components require strong writing skills.

content.why_this_matters:
You have mapped your community, served in it for months, studied how your local government works, learned your constitutional rights, and practiced leading and organizing groups. Each of those experiences taught you something specific. Together, they have formed something larger: you are becoming a person who pays attention, who contributes, and who understands how the systems around you work.

This capstone packet pulls it all together. You will build a civic portfolio — a living document that shows what you have done, what you have learned, and how your understanding of civic life has grown. And you will design and execute a community contribution project that integrates multiple skills from across this pillar.

The portfolio is not a binder of certificates. It is not a collection of photos from volunteer events. It is a reflective record that demonstrates genuine engagement with your community — the kind of engagement that most adults never achieve and that you, at your age, are already practicing.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Civic portfolio development** — documenting and reflecting on engagement across all 4.5 sub-domains
2. **Cross-domain integration** — understanding how the different components of civic engagement connect and reinforce each other
3. **Community contribution project design and execution** — a real project that produces real outcomes for real people
4. **Reflective civic practice** — the habit of thinking critically about what you have done, what worked, and what you have learned
5. **Emerging civic identity** — the growing sense that civic engagement is part of who you are, not an assignment you complete
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Building the Civic Portfolio

Your civic portfolio documents your engagement across all of 4.5's sub-domains. It is not assembled at the end — it is built continuously, updated as you gain new experiences, and revised as your understanding deepens.

**Section 1: Community Profile (from 4.5.1-B)**
Include your community profile — the asset inventory, needs assessment, power map, and information ecosystem analysis. Update it with anything new you have learned since you first created it. Communities change, and your profile should reflect current reality.

**Section 2: Service History (from 4.5.2-B)**
Document your sustained service commitment: where you served, how long, what you did, what you learned. Include specific reflections — not just "I volunteered at the food bank" but "I served at the Community Food Bank every Tuesday from September through March. I sorted donations, stocked shelves, and distributed bags to families. I learned that the food bank serves 200 families per week and is primarily funded through grants and individual donations. The hardest part was the weeks when we ran low on fresh produce. The most meaningful moment was when a regular family brought us cookies to say thank you."

**Section 3: Civic Knowledge (from 4.5.3-B and 4.5.4-B)**
Include your public meeting account, your budget analysis, your understanding of local government structure, and your constitutional rights knowledge. This section demonstrates that you understand how the systems around you work — not in theory, but in your specific community.

**Section 4: Leadership and Advocacy (from 4.5.5-B)**
Document your meeting facilitation experience, your case presentation, and your sustained group effort. Include reflections on what you learned about leading people, handling conflict, and sustaining effort.

**Section 5: Reflective Essay**
Write a 500+ word reflection connecting the sections: How does community mapping inform where you serve? How does understanding government enable more effective advocacy? How does knowing your rights protect your civic participation? How does leadership make all of it more impactful? The student who can explain these connections has begun to see civic engagement as an integrated system, not a collection of separate activities.

### Part 2: The Community Contribution Project

This is where the portfolio moves from documentation to demonstration. You are going to design and execute a project that integrates multiple 4.5 competencies to produce a real outcome for your community.

**The design process:**

**Identify a need (4.5.1).** Use your community profile. What genuine need have you identified that you are positioned to address? Be specific.

**Design a response (4.5.2 + 4.5.5).** What will you do? Who will you recruit to help? What logistics are involved? What resources do you need? Set a realistic scope and timeline.

**Engage civic systems if relevant (4.5.3).** Does your project require permits, government cooperation, or institutional support? If so, navigate those systems.

**Execute (4.5.5).** Lead the effort. Coordinate people. Solve problems. Deliver results.

**Document and evaluate.** What happened? How many people were served? What worked? What did not? What would you do differently?

**Examples of community contribution projects:**
- Organizing a regular community meal program at a church or community center
- Creating a neighborhood tool library (collecting and lending tools for community use)
- Establishing a tutoring partnership between older and younger students
- Leading a campaign that results in a specific municipal action (a crosswalk, a park improvement, a policy change)
- Building and maintaining a community garden
- Creating a community resource guide that helps residents access services they do not know about

**The critical requirement:** The project must produce a real outcome. Meals actually served. Resources actually distributed. Improvements actually made. A project that exists only as a plan or a presentation has not met the standard.

### Part 3: Cross-Domain Reflection

The final component of the capstone is a reflection that demonstrates you understand how the pieces of civic engagement fit together.

The student who maps a community and then serves in it understands need from the inside.
The student who serves and then studies government understands why policy matters.
The student who knows their rights and then advocates for a position does so with confidence and precision.
The student who leads a group and then encounters institutional resistance learns that leadership requires patience as well as initiative.

These connections are not obvious. They emerge only through the experience of doing all of these things — not in theory, but in practice. Your reflection should demonstrate that you see the connections and that they have changed how you think about civic life.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Portfolio Quality
**Developing well:** Each section demonstrates genuine engagement — specific details, personal reflections, evidence of direct experience. The portfolio is regularly updated, not assembled at the last minute. The community profile reflects current conditions.

**Developing slowly:** The portfolio is thin, generic, or assembled hastily. Sections are copied from assignments rather than reflecting on experiences. No updates since original creation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been building the portfolio continuously, or was it assembled all at once? Continuous building produces depth; last-minute assembly produces filler.

### 2. Community Contribution Project
**Developing well:** The project addresses a genuine community need identified through the student's own mapping work. It produced documented, real-world outcomes. The student can describe the design, execution, challenges, and results with specificity.

**Developing slowly:** The project was planned but not executed, or executed minimally with no measurable outcomes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Was the project scoped realistically? A small project executed well is better than an ambitious project that collapsed. Did the student have adequate support for logistics?

### 3. Cross-Domain Integration
**Developing well:** The student can articulate how community mapping, service, civic knowledge, rights literacy, and leadership connect and reinforce each other. The reflective essay demonstrates genuine understanding, not just description.

**Developing slowly:** The student treats each sub-domain as isolated. No understanding of how the pieces fit together.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student experienced the connections firsthand? The student who used their community map to select a service site and then encountered a government process while executing a project has experienced the integration viscerally. If the sub-domains remained separate in practice, the connections will remain abstract in reflection.

### 4. Civic Identity
**Developing well:** The student shows signs of genuine civic identity — engagement that continues without external prompting, concern for community affairs expressed spontaneously, a sense of belonging and responsibility toward the place they live.

**Developing slowly:** Engagement is still requirement-driven. The student serves, maps, and advocates because it is assigned, not because it is part of who they are.

**Diagnostic questions:** What does the student do when no one is watching? Do they notice community needs without being prompted? Do they talk about their community with ownership and concern? Identity shifts are gradual — look for direction, not completion.
